title: Virginia Man-Yee Lee - Wikipedia
excerpts: **Virginia Man-Yee Lee** ( Chinese : 李文渝 ; born 1945) is a Chinese-born American biochemist and neuroscientist who specializes in the research of Alzheimer's disease . She is the current John H. Ware 3rd Endowed Professor in Alzheimer's Research at the Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine, and the director of the Center for Neurodegenerative Disease Research and co-director of the Marian S. Ware Alzheimer Drug Discovery Program at the Perelman School of Medicine , University of Pennsylvania . <sup>[[ 4 ]]()</sup> She received the 2020 Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences . <sup>[[ 1 ]]()</sup>

title: Local molecular and connectomic contributions of tau-related neurodegeneration - PMC
excerpts:
Section Title: Local molecular and connectomic contributions of tau-related neurodegeneration > Introduction
Content:
In a transition toward a biological, rather than clinical, definition of AD, the National Institute on Aging–Alzheimer’s Association (NIA-AA) has recently established a biomarker-driven classification known as the A/T/(N) framework  [1 ]. These criteria designate labels indicating the presence ( +) or absence ( −) of beta-amyloid plaques (A), tau-based neurofibrillary tangles (T), and neurodegeneration (N). Neurodegeneration in AD is known to be mostly driven by tau neurofibrillary tangles, with substantial research supporting a robust spatial and quantitative correlation between measures of tau and neurodegeneration  [6 – 8 ]. Nevertheless, both tau and neurodegeneration exhibit variability in their distribution across the brain and among individuals, and the relationship between tau pathology and neurodegeneration is not defined by a complete one-to-one correspondence

title: A changing picture for dementia after decades of research | Penn Medicine
excerpts:
Section Title: A changing picture for dementia after decades of research > Connecting the dots
Content:
In the following years, Virginia Lee and Trojanowski’s work extended beyond Alzheimer's disease. Lee and Trojanowski identified the abnormal protein [alpha-synuclein](https://www.nature.com/articles/42166) , accumulating in areas of the brain known as Lewy bodies, as the primary contributor to Parkinson's disease; and were the first to discover that the abnormal folding of a protein known as TDP-43 causes what is now recognized as frontotemporal dementia, a rare neurodegenerative disorder that primarily affects the lobes of the brain responsible for personality, behavior, language, and decision-making. Lee’s research further demonstrated that cell-to-cell transmission of these pathological proteins explains how each disease progresses.

[Dr. Virginia Man-Yee Lee](https://pathology.med.upenn.edu/department/people/455/virginia-man-yee-lee) is a Professor in Alzheimer’s Research at the University of Pennsylvania Perelman School of Medicine, the Director of the Center for Neurodegenerative Disease Research, and the Co-Director of the Marian S. Ware Center for Alzheimer’s Drug Discovery Program.

Content:
Biochemist Virginia Man-Yee Lee has spent her career unlocking the proteins involved in neurodegenerative diseases. Her secret? Happiness.
